sentences.text: That's right.
And that comes-- me what to, what to say, where to start.
Yes.
That happened in, in 1944, about June, July.
Because when the uprising was in full swing and very serious, I was actually at home in Bratislava, because there were school holidays.
And I was entitled to be home for two months, which was quite all right.
And in these two months of school holidays, the uprising came about, in a very big way, which was unexpected.
And I was caught at home.
But one day, I received a telegram from my headmaster in Ruzomberok, in that country's school.
And the telegram said, John, we need your presence in Ruzomberok immediately.
Please come.
To me, it was a secret message, actually, which the director-- who knew exactly who I am and he was very cooperative-- he wanted-- then I shall come to Ruzomberok in this very serious times.
Because he wanted to involve me in the uprising.
He was involved in the uprising and a lot of other teachers and a lot of our prominent people in that town.
So he, he was actually acting in a good face and making me a favor.
Because as a Jew, he thought, in Bratislava, you are now in danger twice.
Because the uprising caused a lot of problems for the handful of Jews, which, wherever they were.
So I would have been safe in Ruzomberok.
And especially I was keen to fight the Germans.
And he knew my way of thinking.
So I, of course, asked my oldest brother, who was like a head of the family.
I said, what shall I do?
He said, no way, you sit home.
Because he was politically also well-informed, maybe even sometimes better than me, because he had the connections to all the big boys.
And they possibly knew-- his advisors, his friends-- then the uprising will not keep going.
Because it was, after all, a very small scratch in the plans of the mighty Germany, which unfortunately proved to be correct.
So my brother said, no, you are not going.
You are on, on school holidays.
You are a teacher.
You have got full entitlement.
What does he want from you?
The school is not running.
No, you just don't go.
I said, but I have to answer.
No, keep quiet.
And that, actually, was a very good advice, maybe unknowingly.
Because if I would have gone there, I would have been definitely involved in the uprising, physically.
And if I would have sent him a telegram, they would have had evidence, whoever.
Because they were listening to the phone calls, the Germans, all the time.
So in other words, I was just home.
And I didn't know what to do with myself.
We-- I was hiding sometimes, sometimes not.
It all depends how they collected the Jews all the time.
So I-- and that, that helped me to get a different idea how to save myself.
Because the life in Bratislava was really impossible.
And when I was doing the hiding, that was the time when I run away from one of these hiding places and went and was sheltered by this Matushek, that his name was Matushek.
And when he, when he was generous enough to let me be there for one day, for one night-- for two, and I think two months or 2 and 1/2 months, I can't remember exactly how long.
And he said, look, Janko-- which was a pet name for John-- I can't let you here to be all the time.
Because the danger is for you and for me, as well.
But I tell you what I will do.
So he made a proposition to me.
He said, I have my old parents.

They are living in a very small town, in the middle of Slovakia-- which was not the place where I usually was living-- in the middle of Slovakia that was far away from the, the uprising area and far away, also, from Bratislava.
A little village, which had no electricity, no train was going there, completely isolated.
He said, my old parents-- the father was already over 65, mother was also in that age, a little bit effected mentally.
Both of them, they were [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They were poor people, retired.
And they were the parents of this good-hearted fellow.
He said, I will put you on the train tomorrow morning.
And you will go to them.
If you succeed to, to come to their village-- mind you, the last station of the train which brought me to that area was about 11 kilometers walking, in a small hillside country, to find that village.
There was no train going there.
You had to walk.
So I went to the nearest town.
Yeah, but how I came to the train, because I told him, look, I should go by train?
They were full of soldiers and full of guards.
That was not just buy a ticket and go on the train.
Because even with the false papers, I wasn't safe.
So he said, don't worry, and I will arrange it.
I will put you on the train safely.
When you are on the train, you are on your own, and you have my address.
When you come to this town, there you get off the train, and we'll walk.
So what?
That will be late in the afternoon.
And he gave me a, a little paper, a message to his father.
He said, when you come there, you just knock on the door, give him this message or read it-- because I don't think they were able to read.
Just tell them who sent you.
He said the son-- your son sends me to you and blah, blah.
And we discussed some story what I will tell them.
That was Hamai.
I changed my name.
Through the underground, through the Mimi and through him.
Because the, the name, Hamai, was my, my last one.
Yeah.
Yes.
That, that-- we had to, we had to hide whenever it was possible.
You know, that was a period which was so uncertain that we didn't know what to do, and we didn't know what will happen.
I was Hamai.
Hamai, Hamai.
H-A-M-A-I.
No, that was my last.
My last, my last.
Hanak.
I can't remember how long that I-- that was my first paper in-- could have been in '40.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember exactly how long.
From the military service, when I didn't return, you know?
Then I started to, to live on different names.
That, that I applied on my own name.
Because I couldn't apply for the job on a false name.
They would have said, who are you?
And on Hirschmann, luckily he knew me from my studying.
But I produced-- oh, that is a good question and good document.
When I applied for the job, I stated in the applications that I am a absolvent.
Because the school was divided in four years.
Two years was like a living certificate and higher class.
And I had the living certificate from the, from the two years.
And I enclosed not a photocopy-- because photocopies were not in use-- but some sort of copy, which was verified by a notary.
And I enclosed it to the Ministry of Education.
So they knew that I had some sort of classification, otherwise I wouldn't have a got the job.
So I went there with these papers.
And they-- yeah, and my-- these were original papers, made for Hirschmann.
So I had to reverse all my stories to my original name.
So when you left to become being an instructor, and you went back to Bratislava--
I was hiding, I was living in hiding in Bratislava.
Up to the time when I, when I was also hiding with Matushek, that was '44.
And then, when, when I couldn't stay there anymore, then he suggested that I will help you to go away to the country.
Because it was impossible.
The whole area, any country area, was isolated from the capital territory, like Sydney area was completely under German jurisdiction.
But if you went, let's say-- not Penrith, further-- Newcastle.
Newcastle district was less controlled.
Metropolitan, that's right.
But this one was not only metropolitan.
That was about 200 kilometers, 150 kilometers.
It was an area of Slovakia which was not politically or economically very important.
Therefore there were villages which were very primitive, you know.
And that was the village where his parents were living.
And he, he took me one, on morning, very early in the morning.
And what, what is coincidence and sometimes luck, I got a terrible cold.
It was in the severest winter in '44, in that time.
And I got a terrible cold, and I had such colds and I was swollen in the nose and everything.
Maybe I had inflammation of some sort, my face was unrecognizable.
And that was the day when he said tomorrow you-- Janko, tomorrow I have to take you.
And you will go, by yourself, to Topolcany.
Topolcany was the main town.
If you mention it to somebody who knows the geography, they said, you went from Bratislava in '44 to Topolcany?
It's unbelievable.
But I did it.
Why?
Because he, he dressed me up in his old military coat.
He gave me also the cap and the scarf, woolen scarf.
And I was so swollen that nobody would have recognized me, if somebody recognized me from the old times. you know.
And that was under his supervision.
And he took me really-- that was early in the morning.
He had, he had that morning was on duty to-- on the main railway station.
Because his duty covered all the railway station.
They controlled people should not smuggle and things.
They smuggled a lot of products.
So he took me there.
It was dark.
And I was covered with the scarf and with the, the green uniform coat, so he had no problem.
Because there were already SS guard and local SS guard guarding the railway.
Because they knew a lot of partisan.
That's when the partisan movement started.
And the restrictions were very severe.
Because they wanted to, to caught a lot of people who collaborated with the uprising.
He managed to put me on the train.
On the train, they were full of soldiers.
But I had the coat up, and I was just somewhere in the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I, I can hear the soldiers talking German and says that we can smell a Jew 100 meters, something like that.
You know, so I said to myself, you can't smell nothing.
Because I'm standing here.
So that, that was a very critical time.
But I survived, because I am here.
But when I came to the main station, the end station where the train-- when I got off, I was really left-- I never before.
Usually, I knew Slovakia very well.
But there were parts, like in every country, where you don't go.
You didn't go to Dobo, maybe, or somewhere much smaller.
So in that part of Slovakia, I never-- I was never involved business-wise or nothing.
So I was stranger there, which was good for me.
But I didn't know where to go and how to go.
So when, when the train stopped on that station, I only dared to ask, how do I come to that village?
The village was called Male Bedzany, which is not important.
They said, oh, you go this way.
And there were only fields, you know, that was full of middle of winter-- covered with snow.
And they just said, walk this way, you will come there, there is nothing else there.
I was walking, how long?
I don't even know, must have been three hours, maybe more.
But my condition, physical condition, was getting worse and worse.
I hardly ate anything.
I didn't-- maybe he gave me bread, the peasant bread, which is a very, very nourishing.
I was not curious if I eat or not.
But I was able-- I was marching, marching.
I came there.
It was maybe 5 o'clock in the afternoon, it was already dark.
And I-- there was a very small village.
And I found out that little house, there was the poorest house in the village, because they were very poor.
And I knocked on the door and collapsed.
I had the paper in my hand what he wrote.
I gave it to the man.
And I can't remember, I was in a daze.
They have seen and he read the message from his son.
Because you know, the poor peasants, who have really nothing, and they have one son who lives in the big town and is educated and is a finance man, so they are very proud.
He's the god in the family.
So they said, [?
Shanko-- ?] [?
Shanko ?] is Alex.
His name was Alex, the parents called him [?
Shanko. ?] And they said, when Alex send him, when [SLOVAK] sends somebody here, of course we have to do the best.
You see the primitive or simple people, peasants, they show a lot of love to somebody who needs it, especially if their family members sends him.
So [?
Shanko ?] sent me there.
And the story was he, he rehearsed it with me.
Tell them you are a student.
You got very sick, I can't keep you here.
How do you know me?
He invented also that.
He said, you are a, a university student.
And you are very poor, because your parents are already in the occupied territory.
And I-- he was making it-- you know, the finance or gendarmes or-- they always have to, if they want to advance, they have to make a special exams, every half a year or maybe one.
So he said you tell my parents we know each other because you give me private lessons for my exams.
And I didn't pay you anything, and now you've got so sick.
So I send you to my parents, to look after you while you are sick.
And I was sick, I had fever.
I was in their bed.
You know, and the peasant beds, they have got huge pillow cases, up to the ceiling.
[LAUGHS] So I was buried in the, in the feather beds.
And they gave me-- they were poor, so they gave me milk.
I was not able to eat anyhow.
But whatever they forced me-- the wife-- I was eating milk-- fresh milk, which they had one cow, hot milk from the cow-- and bread.
And about one week, I was there half dead.
And they brought me back to life.
And they, really they did everything for me.
I can't remember what-- how was the first couple of days.
When I was already halfway through, then I could talk.
So I told them only the best about their son, sends them best regards.
And [?
Shanko ?] is such a good fellow.
They said, yes, yes, he wrote this.
And they didn't mention 'and what will you do?' I was part of their family, you know?
Nobody had any-- especially not in winter.
In winter in a small village, they have nothing much to do.
The fields are covered with snow.
They have one cow or maybe one horse and, and goats, things like that.
Self-sufficient but very primitive.
So I was living there to, to the liberation.
That was from October '44 till, till February-- not February.
January, February, March-- till 2nd of March, I think.
I was liberated in that village.
Who liberated you?
Who?
Russian army.
Yes, that was the worst time ever.
Because when I was living, I was living there from October till my liberation.
That is, what-- November, December, January, February-- almost four months.
The tragedy was then, I, I am not a, a hero by nature.
But out of necessity, I became a hero.
Because when you are living between such simple people in a small village-- they were maybe all together 30 or 40 people, including the children-- so everybody knew each other.
They were poor, they had a very simple, primitive life.
And they were looking after-- I was called I am evacuant.
I am evacuant.
You know what is evacuant?
People who were-- because they were already bombing major cities.
Bratislava was bombed a couple of times and the government tried to send women and children to the country to be safe.
And that was called [NON-ENGLISH], evacuation of a territory.
And people who were evacuated from a territory were called evacuants.
So I was a evacuant officially, for the people.
Because in the village, they are curious.
Who are you and what are you doing here?
So I had to produce some sort of story.
That was the story.
And they said, he, he was teaching our son, [?
Shanko. ?] And he's a very good man, and he helps my son.
So I was the hero.
And the, and the-- no, I was a fellow from a city.
And for the village people, you know, you are from the city, you are weak, and you don't have to work because you don't know how.
So they treated me with utmost respect.
And I had the best-- I mean, primitive life but I couldn't go out unnecessary.
But every week, every week on a Thursday-- Wednesday or Thursday, I'm not sure-- the, the women in that village, they have-- they made their own bread.
And the bread is like this little table, a huge one.
And each family made three or four, some of them five.
So the, the woman of the house, the old one, she carried one big bread.
And they always said, oh, Janko, you are coming with us.
We are going to the, to the nearest town there, where I came by train.
And that was 11 kilometers to walk.
But each-- and they were six or seven or eight women sometimes, carrying the bread to the baker to be baked.
Some of them had a little oven.
But the good one-- and especially when they had more bread.
So they carried it on their back, in a linen cloth and also a little basket.
And they carried the bread, early in the morning, through the fields to the main town.
There, they put it to the bakery for baking.
And in the afternoon, between 2:00 and 4:00 it was ready, and we had to walk back.
And Jan Hamai had to do this job, you know, because that was my cover.
And so I couldn't say, no, I'm not going.
How come?
That would be suspicious.
So I had to play the role, yes, I know what it is, because I come also from a country town.
Then I was walking every week to the country town, to carry the bread.
I knew only that not many people could recognize me.
Because there was always a danger that somebody will recognize my face.

But because I was studying up to '38 and the beginning of '39 in Brno and I didn't come home very often and I changed-- you know, you become from a teenager almost a man, I was already 19 then-- then I thought not many people will recognize me.
Especially not in this district, where I never went before.
And no-- thanks God, nobody recognized me.
But it was, for me, a big tension, you know, to, to be in a town of four hour.
Sometimes more, because they went to shopping, and there was a, a market.
You know, all the, all the peasants, they bring the, their produce there, to sell to get a little bit of money.
So they mingled and they, they have this as a gossip, you know.
And I was always sitting somewhere in the corner.
Because I didn't want to be seen, and that was very hard to tell them why.
Janko, come here, maybe you are cold.
Or you want to go to the pub, to have a beer.
And I always had to find a logical answer.
So it was very nice of them.
But it's also very, very dangerous for me.
So I was living in that village.
And suddenly, you know, you have no idea or no view, how does it look when a front comes and changes the places?
We, we were occupied by Germans.
And about a couple of weeks before I was liberated-- two weeks or maybe more-- they came suddenly early in the morning, one day.
A big high-ho, they came about three lorries, German and also drawn carriages from the neighboring village.
And they had drums, a few, we didn't know what.
It was early in the morning, it was a big high-ho.
The woman came to me and said, Janko, quickly, quickly get dressed.
Because during the day, I was in a shirt or very primitively dressed.
I didn't go out, it was winter.
She said, get quickly dressed.
The Germans came in.
I said, what Germans?
No, I don't know, but the German SS is here with cars.
And they, they said they will-- I looked out from the window and I saw that.
I said, but they have drums?
They said, yes, they have got kerosene.
If they find here partisans, they will burn the village down.
But they-- don't worry, we have no partisans.
[LAUGHS] They were not guilty.
Because many of these villages were on the side of-- and this, luckily, was one of the villages-- they didn't have a Jew for 80 years in that village.
And they were very proud of that.
So this danger was not.
But for me, it was a danger.
Because I knew that if Germans come and occupy a small town or big one, they start to verify each one who lives there.
And really, the next day, there was a drummer going in the small village.
Everybody from the village, including children and old people, must come to the head man's village-- house.
So there were, I don't know, 40 or 50 people, all together.
And we had to line up.
To myself, I said, now, this is it.
John, now you are finished.
Because verification had to take my false papers.
And they-- we were standing in queue, one by one was let in.
And there were sitting two SS men behind the table, the head man of the village, who was like a trustee.
And each person who came in, he said, oh, yes, that's Mr.
Hanak, I know him.
When he-- when it was my turn, I thought, maybe they will give me away.
He didn't.
He was convinced I am Hamai, I am a student, I am a best friend from, from, Alex.
Because they knew each other.
That was like a big family.
So he, he spoke-- before I had the chance to talk anything-- he said, oh, no, I know him, Hamai, he lives-- I think he was a cousin where I was living.
He's all right.
But I had to show the paper.
They looked a it.
[GERMAN] Deutsch?
And I said, [GERMAN] Deutsch, [GERMAN] Deutsch, like a typical Slovak.
Because I was dressed like a peasant.
[GERMAN] Deutsch.
So the one said to the other.
[GERMAN] Deutsch, he said in German.
He doesn't speak German.
So I, I had to talk Slovak to the head man.
And he was translating it.
But I knew every word.
And that was my luck, that I had always a little bit of time to think about the answer.
So he asked me, [GERMAN].
What do you make here?
So I had to give him the same story which I gave to the peasants.
And I am sick, I am a student.
And I came here because I need re-convalescence and they are very good to me, blah, blah.
They didn't have much time to interrogate me.
If he would have been more suspicious, I wouldn't have survived there.
Because that was really-- what he did, and that is exclusive, he gave a stamp on the, on the identity paper, [GERMAN].
That means verified.
And that was, for me, a new passport.
And they were staying.
But unluckily there were two of them-- because they came maybe two dozens of these soldiers-- two of them were, were sent to our house, to sleep there.
They didn't tell us if one night or two nights, we didn't know.
But they came and they had to sleep somewhere.
So I moved to a little-- you know, a peasant house has one bedroom, one kitchen.
I was sleeping normally on the kitchen floor, because there it's hot.
But the two soldiers were sleeping in because the woman with children-- she had two children-- she was in the bedroom.
So I was sleeping in a, in a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That is a little room where they have potatoes and everything.
I didn't complain, I was happy.
I was only worried what the Germans will-- I didn't talk to them, because I couldn't speak German.
And they, they were, they were Austrians, in the age of 50 or 60.
They were old Austrians.
And they were used for this sort of work.
And they were really looking for partisans or for the uprising, the people who were in the middle of the uprising.
And-- but the front came, luckily, so close to our village.
Then we heard already the couple of nights bombardment, day and night, and even, in the evening, flashes of the shots.
And suddenly, one early in the morning, we were occupied by the retreating German army.
They were still very organized.
They had the tanks in the outskirts, not in the streets of the village, because it was too small.
But they were all around the area, because they were retreating.
The Russians, they drove them back.
And I was worried that the change over can be very tragical.
So when I, when I had a chance to talk, during the day, to a couple of the younger man which knew me-- and you know, the peasants in winter, they, they had to do some sort of work.
So they usually carry the manure from the animals to the fields, and they put a big heap.

So I started to talk in such a ways and if it will-- if the change over will be very bad-- because I told them you know, I know from other places, especially where I come from, when the German army retreated, they took all the able men and left only women and children.
And that can happen here.
They said, really?
Because they're a primitive, simple people.
They didn't know.
So I said, maybe we can hide outside in the field.
We will make a hiding place in the manure heaps and straw heaps.
So they said, yes, when it comes to that.
And I started to organize and said we should do it.
Luckily, the occupation was about two days.
The second day was-- must have been a very big attack by Russian army.
So the Germans, in a in a hurry, they evacuated.
They ran, ran away from that village, another 10 miles, or five kilometers.
And the next-- then we were free.
I was actually hiding with the two fellows which I talked into-- I was already on the field.
I said we are going to make the, the hiding place.
But we didn't do it physically.
But we were there, considering it.
And suddenly-- and there was-- the Germans moved out.
And we have seen it from the distance.
And suddenly I noticed coming towards the field-- because that was flat fields there-- I notice that there are coming soldiers, who are different color.
Because German army was in grey, and the Russians were in green.
But that was-- you wouldn't have a clue.
That was Malinovsky army.
That was the third Russian army, which occupied Hungary, part of Yugoslavia, Romania, and they came to Slovakia.
And that's-- Malinovsky army was the third worst supplied army ever, because it consisted of criminal element.
In Russian-- Russia, especially Stalin's regime-- they had no interest.
They put them on the worst spots to decimate them.
Because they were the un-- unwanted element in the Russian army.
They had two best armies, which went over Berlin.
And the other one went through Poland.
So I have seen that this must be already the Russians.
And they had the short machine guns, the [RUSSIAN], which are typical for not for you, but for me I knew it.
So I-- the two peasant boys, they were hiding behind the, the manure and the straw heaps.
And I was the big hero because, for me it was liberation.
And I jumped out, and I put my hands up and started to shout in Russian, don't shoot.
Don't shoot, I am a spy.
And I want to, and I want to see-- because I knew that the Russians, they shoot first and ask questions only after.
I had to concoct some sort of a story.
And the first Russian soldiers who came, really, he had a bayonet on his gun.
And he swore to high heaven.
And he said, you are spy?
And he said very nasty words, which I understood.
You see, when you speak Slovak, you can understand Polish, you can understand also Russian.
Not completely, but-- Did he believe you?
No, he didn't.
He said, if you are a spy, I give you a bullet in your bloody head.
And that will finish you off.
I said, no, if you do that, you will be in big trouble because I have special news for your commander.
And I said, bring me to you-- show me the way or take me to the commander.
Come on, I go with my hands up.
But bring me to your commander.
He was afraid.
Because an ordinary soldier who fights in the first line, you have no idea how they are.
They are half drunk, otherwise they wouldn't go with a, with a gun against a tank.
And the Germans had first class army, and this one was third class.
They hardly had-- for instance, he didn't have the jacket of military unit.
No, he had the jacket, but the trousers were civilian trousers.
And, and on the back, he had a big bag with his [NON-ENGLISH] for his food.
Because they were the most primitive army.
I felt sorry for them, later on.
Anyhow, he didn't want to show me or guide me.
I said, you take me to your commander.
Because I have very important news for your commander.
He didn't want to.
But then he thought-- I threatened him in a way and said, if you don't do that, you will be in trouble.
So he said, all right, I take you to my next-- who was, I think a sergeant or somebody.
Because to have a captain, you don't find him in the first line.
The captain is behind the line.
I was lucky.
He brought me to a soldier who was willing to take me to the outskirts of the village, where they had a little command post, a small one for that, for, for that unit.
And when he brought me to this hut, where the commander of this section was, he was a Jewish fellow, luckily.
And he, he had somebody in the room, other soldiers.
He sent him out, he closed the door.
I didn't know what he wants from me.
I didn't know he's Jewish.
He said you are the-- you are [RUSSIAN].
I said I'm-- yeah, I made the mistake.
I said to the soldier when he came towards me, I said, I am [NON-ENGLISH].
I had my hands up, I am [NON-ENGLISH].
That means I am Jew, don't shoot.
And I didn't know, that time, that the word [NON-ENGLISH], which means Jew, is a swear word in Russian.
And by law, in Russia, the ordinary peasants were not allowed to say to somebody, you Jew, because it was a swear word.
The order by the government, not so, so much friendliness towards the Jews, but to make them more obedient, they said, you call them [RUSSIAN].
So the, the officer, the officer told me, don't say, [NON-ENGLISH], you say [RUSSIAN].
Therefore, he didn't want to obey you.
Because we don't say the word [NON-ENGLISH].
If you would have said, I am [RUSSIAN].
I said, all right, so what's the difference?
He said, it's a big difference.
Because you are the first living Jew, which I have seen from [?
Kharkiv. ?] You know, and he cried.
So that was a very emotional get together.
And then I said, I want to go home.
I want to go to Bratislava.
Give me some sort of paper, I should go.
He said, you don't need any paper.
You just go and get a lift from the military transport.
I said, but they will not give me a lift just like-- I thought that is a organized-- my foot, they couldn't care less.
He said, just go, don't worry.
Tell them where you want to go, and tell them you are a [RUSSIAN].
But sometimes don't say [RUSSIAN], because they are not very friendly, either.
So this is the finito.
